During glycolysis, locally stored muscle glycogen, and possibly some blood-borne glucose, supply the substrate for energy generation. Glycolysis takes place in the cytoplasm, where no oxygen is required, so the process is called anaerobic. It has been referred to as 'anaerobic lactic', since lactic acid is formed as the end product. Sufficient lactic acid formation can lower the pH of the cell (i.e. make it more acid) to the extent that further energy production may be reduced.

The major substrate for anaerobic glycolysis (see equation below) is glycogen stored within the muscle, so prior hard exercise without adequate repletion of glycogen will limit further high-intensity short-term work.

## Definition

Anaerobic glycolysis is the cytoplasmic catabolic pathway in which muscle glycogen (and potentially blood-borne glucose) is broken down to generate ATP without the use of oxygen, with lactic acid produced as the end product. Because of this end product, the pathway is also referred to as 'anaerobic lactic'. It is one of several interrelated carbohydrate pathways in exercise metabolism, alongside glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis, and sits within a broader chapter-level treatment of the role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise that spans monosaccharide and polysaccharide structure, glycogen granule organisation, the link reaction, the TCA cycle, electron transfer, oxidative phosphorylation, and the formation and fates of lactic acid. The chapter's stated learning objectives frame anaerobic glycolysis within a coherent set of competencies: elucidating the role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise; drawing the general structure of a glucose molecule and its isomers; drawing the general structure of a fructose molecule; describing the formation of a disaccharide and illustrating how and where the glycoside bond is present; outlining the variations in polysaccharides; appreciating the structure of glycogen granules; describing the processes of glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis; describing the formation and fates of lactic acid; outlining the key reactions in the link reaction and the TCA cycle; explaining the concepts of electron transfer and of oxidative phosphorylation; calculating the number of ATP molecules produced during breakdown of a glucose molecule via either the anaerobic and aerobic systems; and describing the formation of fructose. Glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is the key carbohydrate substrate underpinning glycolysis; it is described as a vital energy source for exercise, and its molecular formula reflects 6 carbon atoms, 12 hydrogen atoms and 6 oxygen atoms, giving a molecular weight of 180 (calculated as (12 × 6) + (1 × 12) + (16 × 6) from the atomic masses of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen, 12, 1 and 16 respectively). The molecular weight of a molecule is defined as the sum of the atomic masses of the elements that make up the molecule, calculated by multiplying the atomic mass of each element by the number of atoms of that element present in the compound and summing the results. When fully oxidised aerobically it reacts with oxygen (C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂ → 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O), whereas under anaerobic conditions its catabolism in the cytoplasm terminates at lactic acid. As a monosaccharide hexose, glucose has a defined general structure and exists in several isomeric forms, and it is the carbohydrate unit liberated from polysaccharide stores (notably glycogen) that feeds the glycolytic sequence. Glucose is introduced as one of two key metabolites in the chapter's framing reactions, paired with a non-esterified fatty acid (palmitate) to illustrate the contrasting stoichiometries of carbohydrate and fat oxidation: glucose + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂ → 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O) and NEFA (palmitate) + oxygen → carbon dioxide + water (CH₃(CH₂)₁₄COOH + 23 O₂ → 16 CO₂ + 16 H₂O). The chapter explicitly introduces glucose by directing the reader to examine these two important reactions side by side, presenting the glucose and NEFA (palmitate) oxidation equations together as the anchoring pair of reactions that involve the two key metabolites of the carbohydrate-versus-fat fuel comparison.

## Core Principles

- Glycolysis occurs in the cytoplasm of the muscle cell and does not require oxygen, hence its designation as anaerobic.
- The substrates for energy generation are locally stored muscle glycogen and, possibly, some blood-borne glucose.
- Lactic acid is the end product of anaerobic glycolysis; its accumulation lowers cellular pH (increases acidity).
- Sufficient lactic acid formation can reduce intracellular pH to an extent that further energy production may be inhibited, providing a self-limiting mechanism for the pathway.
- The ATP yield from breakdown of a glucose molecule via the anaerobic system is substantially lower than that obtained when glucose is fully oxidised aerobically through the link reaction, TCA cycle and oxidative phosphorylation.
- The number of ATP molecules produced during breakdown of a glucose molecule can be explicitly calculated for both the anaerobic and aerobic systems, providing a quantitative learning outcome that distinguishes the two energy pathways.
- Complete aerobic oxidation of glucose follows the stoichiometry C₆H₁₂O₆ + 6 O₂ → 6 CO₂ + 6 H₂O, contrasting with the partial, oxygen-independent breakdown achieved by anaerobic glycolysis; this places glucose alongside non-esterified fatty acids (e.g. palmitate, CH₃(CH₂)₁₄COOH + 23 O₂ → 16 CO₂ + 16 H₂O) as a key oxidisable substrate, but only carbohydrate substrates can sustain ATP generation when oxygen is unavailable.
- Glucose and NEFA (palmitate) are presented together as the two key metabolites whose paired oxidation reactions frame the energetic comparison between carbohydrate and fat as fuels.
- The paired glucose and palmitate oxidation reactions are explicitly framed in the chapter as the two important reactions to be examined together when introducing glucose, reinforcing that they jointly define the two key metabolites underpinning fuel-selection discussion.
- Glucose's molecular composition (C₆H₁₂O₆: 6 carbon, 12 hydrogen and 6 oxygen atoms) and its derived molecular weight of 180 g·mol⁻¹ — obtained by summing the atomic masses of its constituent elements (C = 12, H = 1, O = 16) multiplied by their respective atom counts — provide the quantitative basis for stoichiometric calculations of substrate use and ATP yield in glycolysis.
- Molecular weight is calculated from the molecular formula by multiplying each element's atomic mass by the number of atoms of that element in the compound and summing the products; applied to glucose this gives (12 × 6) + (1 × 12) + (16 × 6) = 180.
- Carbohydrate substrate availability for glycolysis is shaped by the broader carbohydrate hierarchy: monosaccharides (e.g. glucose, fructose) combine via glycoside bonds to form disaccharides, which extend into polysaccharides of varying structure; muscle glycogen, the principal in situ polysaccharide store, is organised into granules whose architecture supports rapid mobilisation into the glycolytic pathway.
- Anaerobic glycolysis is one element of an integrated set of carbohydrate processes — glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis — together with the formation and fates of lactic acid, the link reaction and TCA cycle, and electron transfer coupled to oxidative phosphorylation, all of which jointly determine ATP yield from a glucose molecule via the anaerobic and aerobic systems.
- Fructose, like glucose, is a hexose monosaccharide with its own general structure, and is itself produced via specific formation pathways within carbohydrate metabolism.

## Sub-concepts

- **Substrate sources**: muscle glycogen (major) and blood-borne glucose (possible secondary contribution).
- **End-product accumulation**: lactic acid as both signature product and limiting factor via pH reduction.
- **Glycogen depletion effect**: prior hard exercise without adequate glycogen repletion will limit subsequent high-intensity short-term work, since muscle glycogen is the major substrate.
- **Fates of lactic acid**: once formed, lactic acid has multiple metabolic fates beyond simple accumulation, linking anaerobic glycolysis to broader carbohydrate metabolism.
- **Relationship to other carbohydrate pathways**: anaerobic glycolysis interfaces with glycogenolysis (mobilising glycogen as substrate), glycogenesis (replenishing glycogen stores) and gluconeogenesis (regenerating glucose from non-carbohydrate sources, including lactate).
- **Glycogen granule structure**: muscle glycogen is stored in granules whose structure underpins its rapid mobilisation as substrate for glycolysis.
- **Glucose as the central monosaccharide substrate**: glucose (C₆H₁₂O₆) is the immediate hexose unit derived from glycogen that enters the glycolytic sequence, and its contrasting aerobic vs anaerobic fates define the energetic context of the pathway.
- **Molecular formula and molecular weight of glucose**: glucose contains 6 carbon, 12 hydrogen and 6 oxygen atoms (C₆H₁₂O₆); its molecular weight is the sum of atomic masses of its constituent elements multiplied by their numbers of atoms — (12 × 6) + (1 × 12) + (16 × 6) = 180 — establishing glucose as a vital exercise energy source with a quantitatively defined mass per mole.
- **General method for calculating molecular weight**: starting from a molecular formula, the atomic mass of each constituent element is multiplied by the number of atoms of that element present in the compound, and the products are summed; this generic procedure underlies the worked calculation for glucose and is generalisable to other metabolites encountered in exercise biochemistry.
- **Glucose and NEFA as paired key metabolites**: glucose and palmitate (a representative NEFA) are explicitly framed together as the two key metabolites whose oxidation reactions anchor the discussion of fuel use; the chapter introduces glucose by inviting the reader to examine the two important reactions (glucose + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O and palmitate + O₂ → CO₂ + H₂O) in parallel, establishing this paired-equation presentation as the structural device for comparing carbohydrate and fat oxidation.
- **Monosaccharide structure and isomerism**: glucose has a characteristic general structure and several isomeric forms; fructose, another hexose monosaccharide, has its own general structure and is itself the product of specific formation pathways within carbohydrate metabolism.
- **Disaccharide and polysaccharide architecture**: monosaccharide units are joined through glycoside bonds to form disaccharides, and further linkage gives rise to polysaccharides that vary in structural arrangement, with glycogen representing the storage polysaccharide most relevant to muscle energy provision.
- **Aerobic vs anaerobic ATP accounting**: the full breakdown of a glucose molecule yields a calculable number of ATP molecules differing markedly between the anaerobic system (terminating in lactic acid) and the aerobic system (proceeding through the link reaction, TCA cycle, electron transfer and oxidative phosphorylation).
- **Aerobic terminal machinery**: the link reaction and TCA cycle furnish reduced cofactors whose electrons are passed along the electron transfer chain, driving oxidative phosphorylation as the principal ATP-generating step that distinguishes aerobic from anaerobic glucose catabolism.
- **Role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise**: carbohydrates serve as the central fuel class whose structures (glucose, fructose, disaccharides, polysaccharides, glycogen granules) and pathways (glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, glycogenesis) collectively support energy provision across exercise intensities.
- **Chapter learning objectives**: the coverage of anaerobic glycolysis is anchored by an explicit objective set requiring learners to elucidate the role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise; draw glucose and fructose general structures and glucose isomers; describe disaccharide formation and the location of the glycoside bond; outline polysaccharide variations; appreciate glycogen granule structure; describe glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis; describe the formation and fates of lactic acid; outline key reactions in the link reaction and TCA cycle; explain electron transfer and oxidative phosphorylation; calculate ATP yield from glucose breakdown via the anaerobic and aerobic systems; and describe the formation of fructose.

## Practical Applications

- Adequate repletion of muscle glycogen between bouts of hard exercise is essential to sustain high-intensity short-term work capacity.
- Recognising lactic acid–induced pH reduction as a limiter of energy production informs pacing and recovery strategies during repeated high-intensity efforts.
- Understanding the comparatively low ATP yield of anaerobic glycolysis versus aerobic oxidation of glucose helps frame the role of carbohydrates in sport and exercise, particularly in selecting fuelling and training strategies for events of differing intensity and duration.
- Appreciating that glucose can be either fully oxidised (when oxygen supply matches demand) or partially catabolised to lactic acid (when it does not) helps practitioners interpret the shift from aerobic to anaerobic energy provision as exercise intensity rises.
- Quantifying the ATP yield from glucose breakdown via the anaerobic versus aerobic systems supports rational prescription of carbohydrate intake and training emphasis according to the predominant energy system of a given sporting task.
- Comparing the paired oxidation reactions of glucose and palmitate (NEFA) clarifies why carbohydrate becomes the obligatory fuel under anaerobic conditions, supporting fuel-selection reasoning across exercise intensities.
- Using the molecular weight of glucose (180 g·mol⁻¹), derived from its atomic composition C₆H₁₂O₆, allows practitioners and scientists to convert between mass and moles of carbohydrate when calculating substrate utilisation, energy yield and carbohydrate intake recommendations during exercise.
- Applying the general molecular-weight procedure (multiplying each element's atomic mass by its atom count in the formula and summing) lets practitioners derive the molar mass of any carbohydrate or metabolite of interest from its molecular formula, supporting consistent stoichiometric reasoning across substrates beyond glucose.
- Mapping the integrated sequence of glycogenolysis → glycolysis → link reaction → TCA cycle → electron transfer → oxidative phosphorylation, alongside gluconeogenesis and glycogenesis, gives practitioners a coherent framework for explaining how dietary and stored carbohydrate is converted into ATP during sport and exercise.
- Using the chapter's learning-objective set as a structured checklist — covering carbohydrate structures (glucose, fructose, disaccharides, polysaccharides, glycogen granules), the core pathways (glycogenolysis, glycolysis, gluconeogenesis, glycogenesis), lactic acid formation and fates, the link reaction, TCA cycle, electron transfer, oxidative phosphorylation, and ATP-yield calculation via anaerobic and aerobic systems — supports systematic teaching, study and curriculum design around anaerobic glycolysis in sport and exercise biochemistry.
